name: Non-idempotent retry on stateful operations
trigger_shapes: retry around a database write, payment, or email send
retry policy that does not distinguish retryable from non-retryable errors
operation that creates a record or charges a customer wrapped in generic retry logic
message consumer that reprocesses messages without an idempotency key check
activation: A retry is triggered on an operation that has already partially or fully committed state (e.g., a payment was charged, a record was created, an email was sent) due to a transient error in the response path — the operation succeeded but the caller never received confirmation.
mechanism: Operation executes and commits state; a network failure prevents the success response from reaching the caller; caller's retry policy triggers a second execution; the second execution duplicates the side effect (double charge, duplicate record, second email); neither execution has an idempotency key check to detect the duplicate; reconciliation detects the inconsistency hours or days later.

signal_sequence.signal: Retry metrics record a 'success' on the second attempt. Error rate returns to zero. No alert fires.
Duplicate entries appear in audit logs or database. Counts in reconciliation reports don't match expected totals.
Inconsistent state detected across systems. Finance or operations team flags discrepancies in reports.
Customers report overcharges, duplicate orders, or receiving the same notification multiple times.

threshold_estimated: More than 3 retries per operation without idempotency keys. Based on the default max_attempts in common retry policies (boto3, requests, urllib3). Even a single retry without idempotency on a payment operation is unacceptable.
invisible_because: The retrying operation looks retry-safe in isolation — the call signature is the same, the retry policy is standard. Non-retryable error types (e.g., ValueError) are not explicitly listed in the policy, so all failures look retryable. The happy path (no failure) is tested extensively; failure-during-response is not.
diagnostic: Are non-retryable error types (e.g., any error from a successful write that fails on response) explicitly excluded from the retry policy? Does every state-mutating operation require an idempotency key that is checked before execution, not just passed through?
countermeasures: Require idempotency keys for all state-mutating operations; check the key at the start of execution and return the prior result if found.
Explicitly enumerate non-retryable error types in retry policies — do not use a generic catch-all.
Use the outbox pattern: write intent to a durable store before executing; deduplicate on the consumer side.
Test the failure-during-response path explicitly: simulate a successful write followed by a dropped response and verify the retry produces no duplicate.
For payments specifically: use payment provider idempotency keys (Stripe, Braintree all support this natively).

sources.title: Temporal.io — The Golden Rule of Retries
Yandex incident story on retry budgets and duplicate side effects
